nativeRange.habitat: Shallow to mid-depth tropical coral reefs and reef slopes with moderate water movement; occurs on hard substrate in areas of moderate light and access to planktonic food

decorPreferences: open spacing from neighboring corals (15+ cm clearance)
stable mounting surface — wall hammer is top-heavy and must be secured

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae symbiosis. Supplemental target feeding 1–2 times per week with small meaty foods — mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, micro-plankton, or zooplankton-based coral foods — is generally advised to support growth and coloration. Polyps tend to extend during or after lights-out to capture food.

goodWith: reef fish (clownfish, gobies, blennies, peaceful wrasses)
other Hammer Coral (Euphyllia ancora) colonies with adequate spacing
Frogspawn Coral (Fimbriaphyllia divisa / paradivisa) with adequate spacing
non-aggressive LPS corals placed well out of sweeper tentacle range
avoidWith: Torch Corals (Euphyllia glabrescens) — documented to aggressively sting and damage hammer corals
any coral or invertebrate within sweeper tentacle range at night
large angelfish that may nip at polyps
butterflyfish known to nip at LPS polyps

summary: The Wall Hammer Coral is a striking LPS coral bearing distinctive T-shaped or anchor-shaped tentacle tips that sway hypnotically in gentle current. Generally considered intermediate in difficulty; demands stable chemistry and generous spacing from neighbors due to long, potent sweeper tentacles.
careNotes:
TAXONOMY NOTE: Euphyllia ancora was described by Veron & Pichon in 1980 and was reclassified in 2017 to Fimbriaphyllia ancora following molecular phylogenetic analysis that elevated the subgenus Fimbriaphyllia to full genus status and established family Euphylliidae. The trade name Euphyllia ancora remains dominant in aquarium retail and is preserved as the primary scientificName here, consistent with the user-specified trade-dominant convention; Fimbriaphyllia ancora is listed in alsoKnownAs and documented here for accuracy.

SPECIES DISTINCTION — WALL vs. BRANCHING HAMMER CORAL:
Euphyllia ancora (Wall Hammer, this entry) has a continuous, fused flabelloid base — the colony grows as a single merged mass. Euphyllia parancora (Branching Hammer Coral, sw-coral-005) grows on separate branch stalks that can be individually fragged. Wall hammer is generally considered somewhat more difficult to propagate (requires sawing through the skeleton rather than snipping a branch) and tends to reach larger colony sizes over time. The T-shaped or anchor-shaped tentacle tips are identical between the two forms; hobbyists who refer to 'Hammer Coral' may mean either species.

HOBBY DISTINCTION — HAMMER vs. FROGSPAWN vs. TORCH:
• Hammer/Anchor Coral (wall: E. ancora / branching: E. parancora): T-shaped or anchor-shaped tentacle tips.
• Frogspawn Coral (Fimbriaphyllia divisa / paradivisa): rounded, bubble-like tentacle tips resembling frog eggs. Very similar husbandry; considered compatible with Hammer corals and capable of touching without significant stinging.
• Torch Coral (Euphyllia glabrescens): elongated, thin, finger-like tentacle tips. Considered more aggressive than Hammer or Frogspawn — Torch corals placed in proximity have been reported to sting and damage Hammer corals. Hammer and Frogspawn tend to coexist more readily than Hammer and Torch.

In the hobby, all three are often grouped as 'Euphyllia corals' and share broadly similar care requirements. However, Torch corals should be kept well away from Hammers — generally 30+ cm is advised — and should not be relied on to coexist peacefully.

SWEEPER TENTACLES & ALLELOPATHY: Hammer Corals deploy potent sweeper tentacles, typically extending 10–15 cm (4–6 inches) at night, and reportedly up to 20–25 cm when the colony is large or stressed. These tentacles sting and can kill neighboring corals overnight. Spacing of at least 15 cm from any other coral is widely advised; 20+ cm is more conservative. This species is also documented as allelopathic (releasing chemical irritants into the water), so frequent partial water changes and activated carbon are generally recommended in mixed-reef systems with multiple aggressive LPS corals.

LIGHTING: A PAR range of 75–150 is most consistently cited across sources (task specification, ReefChasers, Extreme Corals, BRS). The wall form may be slightly less tolerant of high-intensity lighting than the branching form due to less polyp surface area variation. Acclimating new specimens gradually — starting at the lower end of the range and moving them into target position over 2–4 weeks — is generally advised to prevent bleaching.

FLOW: Moderate, non-laminar indirect flow is preferred. Direct powerhead flow aimed at the coral tends to cause tentacle retraction or tissue damage. Flow sufficient to create a gentle swaying motion of the tentacles is the commonly cited target.

BROWN JELLY DISEASE (BJD): Hammer Corals are considered susceptible to Brown Jelly Disease, a bacterial infection that appears as a brown slimy coating and rapidly dissolves tissue. Physical damage (shipping stress, stings from neighboring corals, direct forceful flow) is the most common trigger. Affected tissue and the surrounding slime should be removed promptly — a fresh- or salt-water dip and mechanical removal of the slime layer are commonly recommended to prevent spread.

WALL HAMMER FRAGGING: Unlike the branching form, wall hammer corals require a saw (bone saw or bandsaw) to propagate, as the skeleton is fused. Individual polyp clusters can be separated along the valley lines of the meandroid structure. This is considered more technically demanding than snipping a branch, and the resulting frags are typically slower to heal.

CALCIUM, ALKALINITY, MAGNESIUM: Multiple sources agree on calcium 400–450 ppm, alkalinity 8–11 dKH, and magnesium 1250–1400 ppm. Sudden alkalinity swings (more than ~0.5–1 dKH per day) can cause polyp recession and bleaching. A two-part dosing regimen or calcium reactor is advisable for established colonies.

CROSS-SOURCE DISAGREEMENT NOTES:
• Temperature: LiveAquaria 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); ReefChasers 77–84°F (25–28.3°C). Averaged to 23–27°C; upper bound capped at 27°C consistent with the branching hammer entry and general tropical reef consensus.
• PAR: BRS/WWC guide suggests 50–150; ReefChasers specifies 100–125 preferred; task specification states 75–150. Range 75–150 adopted as the multi-source average; 75 is appropriate for lower-placed specimens under strong reef lighting.
• Care level: LiveAquaria 'moderate'; ReefChasers 'advanced'; BRS-referenced guides 'beginner-friendly'. 'Intermediate' adopted as the multi-source average, consistent with the branching hammer entry.
• Minimum tank size: Sources range from 40 gal (150 L) for younger colonies to 50+ gal (200 L) for grown colonies. 150 L adopted as minimum with the practical understanding that colonies grow substantially over time.
breedingNotes: Fragging wall hammer corals requires sawing through the fused skeleton, typically with a bandsaw or tile saw, cutting between polyp valleys. This is more technically demanding than fragging the branching form and yields frags that tend to heal more slowly. In the wild, Fimbriaphyllia ancora has been documented as a gonochoric (separate-sex) broadcast spawner; mass spawning events have been observed in Taiwan approximately on the 3rd and 6th nights after a full moon during neap tides in late spring. A 2024 study (Communications Biology) reported an unusual annual sex-change strategy in this species. Deliberate captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the reef aquarium hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Hammer Coral
primary.notes: Primary species care sheet: scientific name Euphyllia ancora; care level moderate; temperature 72–78°F; pH 8.1–8.4; dKH 8–12; SG 1.023–1.025; lighting moderate; flow medium; placement bottom; sweeper tentacles up to 6 inches; weekly feeding micro-plankton or brine shrimp. Family listed as Caryophylliidae — corrected to Euphylliidae per WoRMS 2018.

additional.name: ReefChasers – Hammer Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked: lighting PAR 100–150 (preferred 100–125); flow moderate indirect; aggression aggressive; sweeper tentacles 2–10 inches; feeding occasional spot feeding; temperature 77–84°F; pH 8.0–8.4; alkalinity 8–9 dKH; calcium 400–450 ppm; care level advanced. Taxonomy: family Euphylliidae. Euphyllia-Euphyllia compatibility: Hammer + Frogspawn compatible; avoid Torch proximity. Used for PAR range confirmation and aggression/compatibility detail.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply – A Euphoric Ecoscape of Euphyllias
additional.notes: Cross-checked: PAR 50–150 (low to medium); flow low to moderate indirect; Euphyllia-Euphyllia compatibility confirmed (hammer, frogspawn, anchors, octospawns coexist; torch requires extra space); pH 8.0–8.3; alkalinity 8–12 dKH; calcium 400–450 ppm. Confirmed weekly feeding with small meaty foods. Brown Jelly Disease susceptibility documented.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Euphyllia ancora
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Euphylliidae, order Scleractinia), 2017 reclassification to Fimbriaphyllia ancora, native range (Indo-West Pacific, Maldives to Solomon Islands), depth range (6–40 m), reproductive biology (gonochoric broadcast spawner, neap-tide mass spawning), and IUCN Vulnerable status. Not used for aquarium husbandry parameters.

additional.name: ReefGuide – Anchor Coral (Fimbriaphyllia ancora)
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy: family Euphylliidae, order Scleractinia; accepted name Fimbriaphyllia ancora. Native range: Indo-West Pacific, Australia, Indonesia. Depth: 6–40 m (20–130 ft). Wild colony size: up to 3 ft (approx. 90 cm). Common names: Anchor Coral, Ridge Coral, Hammerhead Coral.
